summary: Scorching Other One and tight Lovelight jam dominate this rock‑forward show
blurb: The second set erupts with Truckin' into a white‑hot The Other One, leading to an epic Turn On Your Lovelight. Jerry’s guitar is crisp and fired up, Phil anchors with in‑charge bass, and Pigpen fuels the jams. Recording captures the energy clearly despite minor tape glitches.
review: On April 12, 1971 at Pittsburgh’s Civic Arena the Dead delivered a high‑octane performance that still feels fresh. After a modest first set, the band launched the second set with a driving Truckin' that segued straight into a relentless The Other One, where Bill Kreutzmann laid down white‑hot drums and Jerry Garcia’s lead was razor‑sharp. The momentum never waned as they weaved through an unforgettable Turn On Your Lovelight, pigpen‑powered harmonica swirling over tight rhythm work from Bob Weir and Phil Lesh. Highlights also include a solid Casey Jones and a bright Sugar Magnolia that closed the night on a high note. The Miller soundboard capture is crystal‑clear, bringing the guitars and vocals up close while preserving enough audience ambience to feel the room’s excitement, though occasional tape glitches shave a few minutes from Hard To Handle.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Positive language across the sole recording praises The Other One, the Lovelight segue, tight Pigpen‑driven jams and strong playing by all members; no consistent negative criticism appears beyond minor tape glitches, warranting a strong positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Crystal‑clear soundboard capture that includes the full performance (aside from minor glitches) and delivers the most detailed mix of guitars, vocals, and Pigpen’s harmonica.

key_highlights: Scorching The Other One with relentless intensity
Epic Turn On Your Lovelight segue showcasing Pigpen’s harmonica
Solid Truckin' launch and tight rhythm section driving the second set

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia’s guitar is fired up, crisp and driving—especially on Hard To Handle, Loser and the marathon The Other One; vocals are energetic though occasionally loose.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh’s bass is in‑charge, prominent in the mix and high‑gear, anchoring the set with solid grooves.
band_performance.Bob: Weir tightens up his rhythm work, driving Hard To Handle and Johnny B. Goode with strong chord work that propels the band forward.
band_performance.Billy: Bill Kreutzmann delivers white‑hot intensity on The Other One and Lovelight, providing a solid, energetic foundation throughout.

must_listen_sequences: Goin' Down The Road Feeling Bad
